samples.self_prompt: The current KMP implementation uses character-level string indexing in Python, which involves creating new string objects for each comparison. Analyze whether converting the text and pattern to byte arrays or integer ordinals before the search loop would reduce per-comparison overhead. Implement the fastest approach and benchmark mentally which change matters most.

The code now uses bytes for fast comparison, but the main loop still iterates character-by-character through the entire text. Investigate whether a hybrid approach using a quick scan for the first character of the pattern to skip ahead could reduce iterations. Also consider whether Boyer-Moore-Horspool might outperform KMP due to its ability to skip multiple characters on mismatches.

samples.code:
def find_all(text, pattern):
    n = len(text)
    m = len(pattern)
    if m == 0:
        return []
    if m > n:
        return []
    text_b = text.encode("latin-1") if isinstance(text, str) else text
    pat_b = pattern.encode("latin-1") if isinstance(pattern, str) else pattern
    if m == 1:
        target = pat_b[0]
        return [i for i in range(n) if text_b[i] == target]
    skip = [m] * 256
    for i in range(m - 1):
        skip[pat_b[i]] = m - 1 - i
    last = m - 1
    results = []
    append = results.append
    i = last
    while i < n:
        j = last
        k = i
        while j >= 0 and text_b[k] == pat_b[j]:
            j -= 1
            k -= 1
        if j < 0:
            append(k + 1)
            i += 1
        else:
            shift = skip[text_b[i]]
            i += max(shift, 1)
    return results
samples.self_prompt: I have optimized data representation (bytes) and added special cases, but have not explored algorithmic alternatives. The KMP algorithm has O(n+m) complexity but its constant factor in Python is high due to the inner while loop. Consider implementing Boyer-Moore-Horspool which can skip large portions of text on mismatches using a bad-character shift table. For typical text with moderate alphabet, BMH often outperforms KMP in practice. Implement BMH and use slice comparison for verification.

The BMH implementation skips well on mismatches but advances by only 1 after every match to handle overlapping patterns. I should compute the pattern minimum period using the KMP failure function so that after a match I can skip by the period length. Also, the inner byte-by-byte comparison loop is slow in Python; replacing it with slice comparison (C-level memcmp) would be much faster. Implement both optimizations.

samples.code:
def find_all(text, pattern):
    n = len(text)
    m = len(pattern)
    if m == 0:
        return []
    if m > n:
        return []
    text_b = text.encode("latin-1") if isinstance(text, str) else text
    pat_b = pattern.encode("latin-1") if isinstance(pattern, str) else pattern
    if m == 1:
        target = pat_b[0]
        return [i for i in range(n) if text_b[i] == target]
    fail = [0] * m
    k = 0
    for i in range(1, m):
        while k > 0 and pat_b[k] \!= pat_b[i]:
            k = fail[k - 1]
        if pat_b[k] == pat_b[i]:
            k += 1
        fail[i] = k
    period = m - fail[m - 1]
    skip = [m] * 256
    for i in range(m - 1):
        skip[pat_b[i]] = m - 1 - i
    tv = memoryview(text_b)
    pv = memoryview(pat_b)
    last_char = pat_b[m - 1]
    first_char = pat_b[0]
    results = []
    append = results.append
    pos = 0
    end = n - m
    while pos <= end:
        c = text_b[pos + m - 1]
        if c \!= last_char:
            pos += skip[c]
            continue
        if text_b[pos] \!= first_char:
            pos += 1
            continue
        if tv[pos:pos + m] == pv:
            append(pos)
            pos += period
        else:
            pos += 1
    return results
samples.self_prompt: The slice comparison approach with BMH skipping and period-aware advancement is good, but can be improved with multi-point filtering. Before the expensive slice comparison, check both the last and first characters. For a small alphabet (4 chars), single-char filters pass ~25% of the time, but a two-char filter passes ~6.25%. Also consider using memoryview to avoid slice copy overhead, and switching to position-based iteration for clearer correctness.
